cards.front: What is SLAAC, and what does it use to discover the prefix?
cards.back: Stateless Address Autoconfiguration. Host sends ICMPv6 Router Solicitation (RS) to ff02::2; router replies with Router Advertisement (RA) carrying the /64 prefix. Host generates the lower 64 bits via EUI-64 (or random per RFC 7217) and uses prefix + interface ID. No DHCP server needed.

cards.front: What's the M flag in an RA, and what does it tell hosts?
cards.back: Managed Address Configuration flag. M=0 (default): use SLAAC for address. M=1: use DHCPv6 for address (stateful). Pair with O flag (Other): if O=1, use DHCPv6 for OTHER info (DNS, etc.) even if SLAAC for address.

cards.front: Configure a Cisco router to act as a DHCPv6 stateless server (SLAAC for addresses, DHCPv6 for DNS).
cards.back:
```
ipv6 dhcp pool DHCPV6
 dns-server 2001:4860:4860::8888
 domain-name example.com
interface gi0/0
 ipv6 nd other-config-flag
 ipv6 dhcp server DHCPV6
```
The `other-config-flag` sets the O bit in RAs.

Stateful: server assigns full IPv6 addresses (M=1 in RA). Server tracks leases. Like DHCP for IPv4. Stateless: SLAAC handles the address; server only provides supplementary config like DNS (O=1, M=0). Cisco supports both.

cards.front: What's the IPv6 default gateway, and how is it learned?
cards.back: Hosts use the link-local address of the router that sent them an RA as their default gateway (NOT a configured GUA). Learned automatically via NDP — no static config needed if SLAAC is in play. Verify on host with `ip -6 route show default` (Linux) or `netsh int ipv6 show route` (Windows).

cards.front: What happens when a host generates an IPv6 address with SLAAC but the address is already taken?
cards.back: DAD detects the duplicate (NS goes out; if NA returns, the address is in use). The host's IPv6 stack marks the address as 'duplicate' and won't use it. Operator needs to investigate (rare in practice with EUI-64, more common with random/manual).

cards.front: List the IPv6 header fields and the size of the header.
cards.back: 40 bytes total (vs IPv4's variable 20-60). Fields: Version (4 bits, =6) | Traffic Class (8, ~ DSCP/ECN, QoS) | Flow Label (20, identifies flows) | Payload Length (16, length of L4 segment) | Next Header (8, like IPv4 Protocol — TCP=6, UDP=17, ICMPv6=58) | Hop Limit (8, like IPv4 TTL) | Source IP (128) | Destination IP (128). NO header checksum, NO fragmentation fields.

cards.front: How is an IPv6 solicited-node multicast address calculated from a unicast address?
cards.back: ff02::1:ff + last 24 bits (last 6 hex digits) of the unicast address. Example: 2001:db8::1234:5678:abcd:1234 → solicited-node = ff02::1:ffcd:1234. Used by NDP NS for L2 address resolution: instead of ARP broadcast, IPv6 multicasts to a small group of hosts whose addresses end in those bits.

cards.front: Map NDP message names to ICMPv6 type numbers: NS, NA, RS, RA.
cards.back: RS (Router Solicitation) = ICMPv6 Type 133. RA (Router Advertisement) = Type 134. NS (Neighbor Solicitation) = Type 135. NA (Neighbor Advertisement) = Type 136. RS sent to FF02::2 (all routers); RA sent to FF02::1 (all nodes); NS sent to the target's solicited-node multicast.

cards.front: IPv6 static-route variants: directly attached, recursive, fully specified — definition and gotcha?
cards.back: Directly attached: only exit-interface specified (`ipv6 route 2001:db8:2::/64 gi0/1`). Recursive: only next-hop specified (`ipv6 route ... 2001:db8::2`). Fully specified: both (`ipv6 route ... gi0/1 2001:db8::2`). GOTCHA: directly-attached IPv6 static routes don't work on Ethernet (router has no L2 mapping for arbitrary destinations). To use a LINK-LOCAL next-hop (fe80::), you MUST use a fully specified route.

The IPv6 equivalent of the ARP table — the NDP neighbor cache: IPv6 address → MAC address mapping, age, state (REACH/STALE/INCMP), and interface. Built from NS/NA exchanges. Same idea as `show arp` for IPv4.
